scriptId: cs_v2_OVERRIDE_L40_Relationship with a boss or coworker
historyId: h_v2_OVERRIDE_L40_Relationship with a boss or coworker

memories.content: An employee reports that their manager interrupts them in meetings, takes credit for their ideas, and micromanages the team, affecting their morale and visibility at work; the respondent offers suggestions for addressing it directly

User asked for top priorities to handle the situation without risking their job. Response provided nine prioritized actions in order:

1) Protect your performance and reputation
2) Document interactions and contributions
3) Make your work visible, proactively
4) Manage meetings and interruptions
5) Prepare for a one-on-one with your manager
6) Reduce micromanagement with clear status and boundaries
7) Build allies and sponsors
8) Escalate only if necessary and carefully
9) Take care of yourself and have contingency plans

It notes focusing first on preserving performance and documenting interactions, then on increasing visibility and a constructive manager conversation, and offers to draft a follow-up email or one-on-one talking points.

User reports a six-month pattern where their team hits targets but cross-team emails omit their contributions, fearing this will negatively affect their next performance review. The assistant responds with understanding and offers to help draft a concise follow-up email to document contributions, provide a template to log incidents, or prepare talking points for a one-on-one with the manager, and asks which option would be most helpful.
User asked for guidance on requesting a one-on-one

User requested two opening lines for a meeting (one direct and one tentative); the response provided two lines: Direct: "Thanks for meeting — I’d like to align on ownership and how my contributions are communicated for [project/team], so we’re clear heading into reviews." Tentative: "Thanks for making time — I was hoping to get your perspective on how I should be communicating my work and to clarify ownership on a couple items so I can better support the team."

A user asked whether to talk to teammates to corroborate a pattern or risk appearing to complain about their manager; the response advised talking to a small number of trusted teammates to seek alignment, not complain, with guidance to: choose peers who’ve been in

The user asked for an email template to summarize the meeting and agreements for a written record, and the assistant provided a template with a subject line, greeting, a confirmation of understanding, a Context section, an Agreements section (Action 1 with Owner and Deadline/Timeline; Action 2 with Owner and Deadline/Timeline; optional process-change item with Owner and Timeline), a Check-in to revisit progress, and closing with a signature and CC line.

- Topic: Suggestions for collaborative frameworks or exercises to make changes feel mutual rather than accusatory.
- Frameworks proposed (with key details):
  1) Start‑Stop‑Continue
     - Fixes: highlights behaviors to keep/change without blaming people.
     - How: 30–45 minute session; each person lists start/stop/continue; synthesize into 3–5 actions with owners.
     - Timeframe: single workshop + 3–4 week review.
     - How to suggest: one-line prompt for a quick session.
  2) Team Working Agreement / Norms
     - Fixes: sets shared expectations for meetings, crediting work, decision‑making, and communication.
     - How: co‑create 5–10 norms; place in team space; remind at meetings; revisit quarterly.
     - Timeframe: 1 meeting to create; continual reinforcement.
     - How to suggest: propose drafting a short working agreement (5 rules) for a quarter.
  3) Meeting norms + facilitator rotation
     - Fixes: reduces interruptions; increases airtime fairness; ensures recaps/owners.
     - How: agree on norms; rotate a meeting facilitator who enforces norms and captures owners.
     - Timeframe: implement immediately; rotate monthly.
     - How to suggest: add a one‑line agenda and rotate

User proposed a meeting role-play to practice handling objections, stating they would send an opening and the assistant would respond as the manager; the assistant agreed to the role-play and asked which tone to use for the manager’s response (defensive/interrupting, dismissive/minimizing, or receptive/constructive) and to send the opening.
The block presents a question about whether it is acceptable to have a trusted colleague sit in on a one-on-one as a witness and whether this
